title: Temperature Converter (Celsius, Fahrenheit, Kelvin, Rankine)
shortDescription: Convert between Celsius, Fahrenheit, Kelvin, and Rankine. All four scales update at once.
description: Type a temperature, choose its scale, and see the equivalent value in the other three scales instantly. Uses exact conversion formulas, no rounding errors.
metaDescription: Free temperature converter for Celsius, Fahrenheit, Kelvin, and Rankine. See all four scales at once with exact conversion formulas — instant, browser-based.

sections.heading: How the conversion formulas work
paragraphs: All four scales pivot through Celsius internally. Fahrenheit ↔ Celsius is F = C × 9/5 + 32 and C = (F − 32) × 5/9. Kelvin shifts Celsius by 273.15 (the absolute zero offset): K = C + 273.15. Rankine is to Fahrenheit what Kelvin is to Celsius — same scale spacing as Fahrenheit, but anchored at absolute zero: R = (C + 273.15) × 9/5.

These formulas are exact: the constants 273.15 (Kelvin offset) and 9/5 (Fahrenheit ratio) are defined by the SI system, not measured. Any rounding you see in the result is from converting to decimal display, not from the calculation itself.

sections.heading: When you'd use each scale
paragraphs: Celsius is the everyday scale almost everywhere except the United States. Water freezes at 0°C and boils at 100°C at sea level — clean, easy boundaries.

Fahrenheit, used primarily in the US, has a finer-grained 'feel' for ambient air temperatures: 0°F is very cold, 100°F is very hot, and most weather sits in the 30-90°F band. The scale was designed around brine and human body temperature in the 18th century.

Kelvin is the scientific standard. It removes the negative-number awkwardness because absolute zero is 0 K, not −273.15. Used in physics, chemistry, and astronomy. Rankine is the imperial-system equivalent of Kelvin — same purpose, but with Fahrenheit-sized degrees. It's still used in some US engineering contexts.

sections.heading: Useful reference points
paragraphs: Absolute zero: 0 K = −273.15°C = −459.67°F. Water freezes: 0°C = 32°F = 273.15 K. Body temperature: 37°C ≈ 98.6°F = 310.15 K. Water boils (sea level): 100°C = 212°F = 373.15 K. Hot oven: 200°C = 392°F. Earth's average surface temperature: about 15°C = 59°F = 288 K.
The Celsius and Fahrenheit scales meet at −40°: that's the same temperature on both. It's the only point where the two scales agree.

howTo.name: Enter the value
howTo.text: Type the number you want to convert.

howTo.name: Pick the source scale
howTo.text: Choose whether your value is in Celsius, Fahrenheit, Kelvin, or Rankine.

howTo.name: Read all four scales
howTo.text: The other three scales update automatically. The selected scale is highlighted.

faq.a: The Celsius-Kelvin offset (273.15) and the Celsius-Fahrenheit ratio (9/5) are SI definitions. Any apparent imprecision is just decimal display rounding.

faq.q: Why does Kelvin not use a degree symbol?
faq.a: By SI convention, Kelvin is written without °. So '300 K' is correct; '300°K' is not.

faq.q: What's the difference between Kelvin and Rankine?
faq.a: Both are absolute scales (zero at absolute zero). Kelvin uses Celsius-sized degrees; Rankine uses Fahrenheit-sized degrees. 1 K = 1.8 R.

faq.q: What's the temperature where Celsius and Fahrenheit are equal?
Why is body temperature 37°C and not 36 or 38?
faq.a: 37°C (98.6°F) is the historical average, but normal body temperature varies by 0.5°C through the day and from person to person. Modern data suggests the average is closer to 36.6°C.
Be careful: a difference of 10°C equals 18°F (you don't add 32). For differences, multiply by 9/5 to go C→F or by 5/9 to go F→C, and skip the offset.

faq.q: Is the calculator accurate enough for science?
faq.a: We display up to four decimals from double-precision math. For metrology-grade work, refer directly to NIST conversion tables, but for chemistry, cooking, and engineering this is more than sufficient.

faq.q: Why include Rankine if it's rarely used?
faq.a: It still appears in some US thermodynamics texts and HVAC engineering work. Including it costs nothing and helps people who actually need it.
